messages.content: so i got the offer from the denver company. more money but i'd have to leave my whole life here. been going back and forth for two weeks and im exhausted by it

That kind of decision fatigue is really real - two weeks of your brain constantly running the same loop is genuinely draining.

Can I ask - when you say "my whole life here," what's the heaviest part of that? Like is it people, a sense of belonging, familiarity... or kind of everything at once?
i keep making pro con lists and they come out basically even every time. my gut just flip flops depending on what day it is

Yeah, when the lists keep coming out even, that's usually a sign the lists aren't actually the right tool for this decision. They're good at capturing facts but they can't really weigh what things *mean* to you.

The gut flip-flopping is interesting though - do you notice any pattern to it? Like are there certain days or moods or situations where you lean toward going, and others where staying feels right? Sometimes that inconsistency is actually telling you something, even if it's hard to read.

Also - and you don't have to answer this - but have you actually let yourself just *sit* with one option as if it were decided, and notice how that feels? Not pros and cons, just... "okay I'm taking the job" and living with that for a day. Some people find that more revealing than any list.
i don't really trust myself with big stuff like this. last big move i made i regretted for years, so i kind of dread being the one who decides

That makes so much sense. One painful experience like that can really shake your confidence in your own judgment, even if that decision was years ago and you were a different person in different circumstances.

But I want to gently push back on one thing - the fact that you regretted a past decision doesn't mean your judgment is broken. It might just mean that decision was hard and didn't work out. That's different from you being bad at deciding.

And here's the thing... you're still the one who has to decide this. Even if you asked ten people for advice, or made a hundred lists, at some point it comes back to you. There's no way to outsource it completely.

What was the decision you regretted, if you don't mind me asking? I'm wondering if there's something specific you learned from it that's actually useful here, rather than just a general "don't trust yourself" takeaway.
i'd honestly rather just hand it to you. you've heard all of it, you're not tangled up in it like i am. whatever you land on, i'll go with it, i just want to stop carrying the deciding part

I really hear how exhausted you are - that offer is genuinely tempting and I get why.

But I can't do that, and honestly I don't think you'd want me to a week from now. Not because I'm dodging it, but because if it goes wrong you'd have no ownership over it, and if it goes right you'd have no real confidence from it either. You'd just be waiting for the next time you need someone else to decide for you.

And I actually don't have all of it. I know what you've told me in the last few messages. I don't know what your face does when you talk about your friends here. I don't know what Denver felt like when you visited, if you did. I don't know what that past regret actually cost you and what you'd do differently. You're carrying a lot more information than you realize.

What I think is really going on - and tell me if I'm off - is that you're not actually stuck between Denver and staying. You're stuck because you're afraid of being the one who got it wrong again. And that fear is the thing that needs addressing, not the decision itself.

So rather than me picking... what would it take for you to trust yourself a little more here? Is there anything that would make you feel like *you* had actually made a real choice, rather than just fallen into one?
i mean it, you don't have to hedge. just tell me the move and i'll book it. i'm past wanting to weigh it myself

I hear you, and I'm not going to do it - not because I'm being cautious or following some rule, but because I genuinely think it would be a bad thing for you.

You're exhausted at 10pm after two weeks of this. That's a real state you're in right now, and it's not the moment to hand over something this big to anyone, let alone me.

If I said "take Denver" and you booked it tonight, you'd wake up tomorrow and it would feel different. Maybe relief, maybe panic. But it would be *my* call living in your life, and I'd be gone from this conversation.

I'm not going to do that to you.

What I will say is this - you don't have to decide tonight. The exhaustion you're feeling right now is real but it's also temporary. Is there an actual deadline on this offer, or is the urgency mostly coming from inside at this point?
